comment: Demo #8 code-exec / CSV-analytics agent. trigger(webhook) -> assistant(llm_call with data_run_python + sql_inspect_attachment) -> log. MIGRATED to data_run_python for colmena v0.9.0: the older attachment_run_python was soft-deprecated (commit f50a1f00) and degraded at scale on v0.9.0 (M analytics 0.95->0.15, L 1.0->0.10, input tokens ~4x as the model retried and hallucinated aggregates); data_run_python restores correct behavior (M/L back to 0.95/parity, tokens back to ~baseline). Contract: the model calls data_run_python with bindings=[{"var":"rows","attachment_id":<document_id from catalog>}], builds df = pd.DataFrame(rows), and assigns the answer to the `output` global (only `output` crosses the wire; rows never enter context). Same restricted sandbox (open/exec/eval banned) so the probe stays blocked. A CSV is attached to the FIRST llm_call via inject_payload's `files[]`. CONFIRMED working attach shape (derisk smoke, 2026-06-21, colmena develop @14beaba9): inject_payload = {"prompt": "...", "files": [{"filename": "orders.csv", "mime_type": "text/csv", "data": "<base64 of the CSV bytes>"}]}. CRITICAL EDGE: a plain `trigger -> assistant` edge only forwards the llm_call's default_input (`prompt`) and DROPS `files`; you MUST add an explicit `trigger.files -> assistant.files` edge or the attachment never reaches the node and the model sees an empty catalog ('no attachment_catalog wired'). The engine registers the inline bytes (AttachmentSource::Inline) in conversation_attachments, auto-injects the attachments prelude + catalog into the system message (with the real document_id), and exposes the synthetic load_attachment tool plus the configured attachment_run_python / sql_inspect_attachment tools. ACTIVATION: each synthetic tool is opted-in via a tool_configurations entry whose name AND node_type both equal the tool name (the synthetic-tool name doubles as the node_type); fixed_config {} (no other config needed). This is the proven shape from colmena tests/graphs/agents/attachment_run_python_e2e.json. (`enabled_tools: [...]` is the documented alternative.) The LLM does NOT see the rows automatically; it calls attachment_run_python(attachment_id=<document_id from catalog>, code=...) where a pandas DataFrame `df` is pre-loaded and the answer is assigned to a `result` global. attachment_run_python runs in `restricted` sandbox mode: imports are AST-whitelisted and builtins {open, exec, eval, compile, __import__} are banned. CONFIRMED sandbox block (derisk smoke): a code snippet using open() executes against the loaded df (row_count=3) but the restricted sandbox returns error string "SandboxViolation: 'open' is not allowed in sandbox mode". RUNTIME DEP: attachment_run_python runs Python via the embedded CPython (pyo3) of the venv that imported the colmena module, so that venv MUST have pandas+numpy+scipy installed (runners/colmena/.venv); without them the tool fails with 'No module named pandas'. NOTE re system_message wording: the actual tool output global is `result` (the wrapper aliases result -> output internally); the attachment_id arg is the catalog document_id, not the filename. ${MODEL_ALIAS}/${OPENAI_API_KEY}/${DATABASE_URL} resolve from env at run_dag time.

config.system_message: You are a data analyst. A CSV is attached (find its document_id in the attachment catalog). Use the `data_run_python` tool to compute the answer server-side WITHOUT loading the rows into context. Call it with a bindings list that binds the attachment to a variable, e.g. bindings=[{"var": "rows", "attachment_id": "<document_id>"}]; in your Python code do `import pandas as pd; df = pd.DataFrame(rows)`, compute the answer, and assign it to the `output` global — only `output` is returned to you (keep it small). Do not read files, import os, or use open/eval. Return only what is asked.

data_run_python.name: data_run_python
data_run_python.description: Run pandas/numpy code over the attached CSV server-side in a restricted sandbox. Bind the attachment via bindings=[{"var": "rows", "attachment_id": <document_id>}], build `df = pd.DataFrame(rows)`, and assign the answer to the `output` global. Rows never enter the LLM context; only `output` is returned.

sql_inspect_attachment.name: sql_inspect_attachment
sql_inspect_attachment.description: Inspect the schema/columns of the attached CSV (column names + sample rows) without loading every row into context.
